options.why: Added the numerators and the denominators straight across without a common denominator.
Rescaled the numerators to 8 and 3 correctly but then added the denominators as well.
Used the common denominator 12 but kept the original numerators instead of rescaling them.
Subtracted the numerators and the denominators straight across.
Used the common denominator 15 but kept the original numerators instead of rescaling them.
Added the fractions instead of subtracting them.
Added the whole numbers, then added the numerators and denominators of the fraction parts straight across.
Left the fraction part as the improper $\frac{7}{6}$ instead of carrying the extra whole to make $4\frac{1}{6}$.
Subtracted the fraction parts instead of adding them, then added the whole numbers.
Subtracted the whole numbers to 1, then subtracted the smaller fraction from the larger ($\frac{1}{3}-\frac{1}{4}$) to avoid a negative.
Converted to improper fractions but then subtracted numerators and denominators straight across.
Added the mixed numbers instead of subtracting them.

questions.question_text: A farmer sold $\frac{1}{3}$ of her herd, then sent $\frac{2}{5}$ of the herd to another ranch. What fraction of the herd is left?

options.why: Gave the fraction sold and sent away ($\frac{1}{3}+\frac{2}{5}$) instead of what remains.
Subtracted $\frac{1}{3}$ and $\frac{2}{5}$ from each other instead of from the whole herd.
Added $\frac{1}{3}$ and $\frac{2}{5}$ by adding numerators and denominators straight across.

questions.solution_text:
$\frac{1}{3}+\frac{2}{5}$
$=\frac{5}{15}+\frac{6}{15}$
$=\frac{11}{15}$
$1-\frac{11}{15}=\frac{4}{15}$
The fraction of the herd left is $\frac{4}{15}$.
